description: Host permissions allow developers to get access based on a specified list of hosts. Host permissions can be any match pattern [1] - “:///*” means "everything", “://.google.com/*” means "all google sites", etc. The risk level of these permissions is dependent on the site it’s requesting access to. If it’s for your corporate site, that poses a higher risk than an extension requesting access to another site such as example.com, which has no user-specific data.

The extension wants to interact with the code running on pages which matches any URL that starts with a permitted scheme (http:, https:, file:, ftp:, or chrome-extension). Can be a broad permission with possible risks.
Can prevent escape button from exiting fullscreen
Capture audio from attached mic or webcam. Could be used to listen in on user
Clears browsing data which could result in a forensics/logging issues
CSP works as a block/allow listing mechanism for resources loaded or executed by your extensions. Can manipulate default CSP.
Could allow plugins to run unsandboxed
P2P communication
Provides high level super user access
The chrome.declarativeNetRequest API is used to block or redirect network requests by specifying declarative rules

chrome.declarativeWebRequest API to intercept, block, or modify requests in-flight. It is significantly faster than the chrome.webRequest API because you can register rules that are evaluated in the browser rather than the JavaScript engine, which reduces roundtrip latencies and allows higher efficiency.
Use the chrome.downloads API to programmatically initiate, monitor, manipulate, and search for downloads. Can be used to download scripts.
Used in conjunction with downloads. Allows an extension to open a downloaded file; as above, can be used to run a downloaded script.
Access to any of the experimental APIs
Access USB devices (can act as driver)
Full history (read, add, delete)
Allows native (Win, Linux, Mac) programs that register with chrome to talk with extensions
Gets MHTML (archived Web page) of any page
Can turn off malware protections (e.g. chrome.privacy.services.safeBrowsingEnabled)
Manage Chrome's proxy settings. Can be used to send user’s internet traffic.
Raw TCP/UDP connection, listen on a port, etc.
Extension wants to interact with the code running on pages which matches any URL that uses the https: or http: scheme. Can be a broad permission with possible risks.
Get picture/video of user’s current tab
Can access current URLs and favicons
Can be used to fetch and execute remote script
Interact with any USB device (Raw Format)
Used in conjunction with USB permission to specify the types of USB devices the extension wants access to
Extension can use webcam, possibly screen contents depending on settings
Extension. can create a VPN tunnel and send/receive packets through it, across sessions
This is an array of strings specifying the paths of packaged resources that are expected to be usable in the context of a web page. This can be used to execute remote scripts.
Listen to the websites that a user visits

Gives an extension temporary access to the currently active tab when the user invokes the extension as when clicking its browser action. For example, with user interaction, the extension can inject JavaScript. Access to the tab lasts while the user is on that page, and is revoked when the user navigates away or closes the tab.
Use the chrome.bookmarks API to create, organize, and otherwise manipulate bookmarks
Read the machine’s clipboard
Writes to the machine’s clipboard
Integrate menus for extension throughout Chrome browser
Lookup or write cookies for any domain specified in permissions (requires host permissions to do this)
Gets screenshot of entire desktop. A private profile extension with this permission could access machine’s data.
Extension could: ● Download file(s) from extension’s specified URL ● Get/modify download history ● Remove downloaded files ● Bypass dangerous download
Read files in folder that user has selected (user interaction required)
Read any file in the directory that a user selects
Retain authorization to previously user selected files
Extension can write files in directory that the user specifies
Write any file in the user specified directory (user interaction required to select file directory)
Get user’s physical location without prompting the user
Extension request oAuth with users credentials - only shows the oauth prompt but does not automatically grant access
Extension can get email address of signed in profile (must be used in conjunction with identity)

Use the chrome.management API to manage the list of extensions/apps that are installed and running. For example, the extension could: ● Uninstall itself ● Disable other extensions ● Check extensions’ state
Use the chrome.processes API to interact with the browser's processes
Use the chrome.sessions API to query and restore tabs and windows from a browsing session. Can keep on re-opening an ad or a bad website.
Save data to user’s drive
List storage devices currently connected and/or eject any connected storage device
Get and/or set list of top visited sites
Text to speech accessibility API
View any GET/POST request & URL (requires host permissions to do this)
Used with webRequest. Allows blocking of a GET/POST request (requires host permissions to do this).
Extension can turn on or off accessibility APIs
Extension can view current state of accessibility features
Extension can set a timer to call its own functions
Window stays on top
Alpha transparency of application window
Application displays over other windows/applications
App can be fullscreen without user interaction
Specify how App window looks
Extension can run when chrome is running
Use this API to expose certificates to the platform which can use these certificates for TLS authentication(s)
Enables actions depending on the content of a page, without requiring permission to read the page's content
Use the chrome.documentScan API to discover and retrieve images from attached paper document scanners
Use the chrome.enterprise.deviceAttributes API to read device attributes. Note: This API is only available to extensions force-installed by enterprise policy.
Use the chrome.enterprise.hardwarePlatform API to get the manufacturer and model of the hardware platform where the browser runs

Use the chrome.enterprise.platformKeys API to generate hardware-backed keys and to install certificates for these keys. The certificates will be managed by the platform and can be used for TLS authentication, network access or by other extension through chrome.platformKeys.
The externally_connectable manifest property declares which extensions, apps, and web pages can connect to your extension via runtime.connect and runtime.sendMessage
Use the chrome.fileBrowserHandler API to extend the Chrome OS file browser. For example, you can use this API to enable users to upload files to your website.
Use the chrome.fileSystemProvider API to create file systems, that can be accessible from the file manager on Chrome OS.
Use the chrome.fontSettings API to manage Chrome's font setting
Extension can send or receive messages via Google Cloud Messaging service

The URL of the homepage for this extension. The extension management page (chrome://extensions) will contain a link to this URL. This field is particularly useful if you host the extension on your own site. If you distribute your extension using the Chrome Web Store, the homepage URL defaults to the extension's own page.
Determine if the system is being actively used, locked, or just not being used
Access media files on local system (requires consent from user)
Use the networking.config API to authenticate to captive portals
Popup notifications to users on desktop
Prevent exiting full screen with ESC

Use the chrome.platformKeys API to access client certificates managed by the platform. If the user or policy grants the permission, an extension can use such a certificate in its custom authentication protocol. E.g. this allows usage of platform managed certificates in third party VPNs (see chrome.vpnProvider).
Manage Chrome OS power state (prevent sleep)
The chrome.printerProvider API exposes events used by print manager to query printers controlled by extensions, to query their capabilities and to submit print jobs to these printers
Use the chrome.signedInDevices API to get a list of devices signed into chrome with the same account as the current profile
Store data on computer
Get physical memory information
Information about processor capabilities
Get info about monitor or change users display settings
Read all text spoken using synthesized speech
Removes HTML5 storage size limits
Use the chrome.wallpaper API to change the Chrome OS wallpaper.
